epigraph.text: Buy truth, and do not sell it; buy wisdom, instruction, and understanding.
epigraph.quote: An estimator is a function of the sample; an estimate is the numerical value it takes.

content.text:
How do we use sample data to estimate unknown parameters? And how 
do we know if our estimator is any good?

content.title: Point Estimator
content.content:
A **point estimator** is a statistic (a 
function of the sample data) used to estimate an unknown parameter.

The value computed from a specific sample is called a **point estimate**.

content.title: Common Point Estimators
content.problem:
| Parameter | Estimator | Notation |
| Population mean $\mu$ | Sample mean | $\hat{\mu} = \xbar$ |
| Population variance $\sigma^2$ | Sample variance | $\hat{\sigma}^2 = S^2$ |
| Population proportion $p$ | Sample proportion | $\hat{p} = \phat$ |

content.text: What makes an estimator "good"? We seek several properties:

content.title: Unbiasedness
content.content:
An estimator $\hat{\theta}$ is **unbiased** 
for $\theta$ if:
\[
 \E[\hat{\theta}] = \theta
\]
The estimator is correct "on average" over all possible samples.

content.title: Consistency
content.content:
An estimator $\hat{\theta}_n$ is **consistent** 
if, as $n \to \infty$:
\[
 \hat{\theta}_n \xrightarrow{P} \theta
\]
The estimator converges in probability to the true value.

content.title: Efficiency
content.content:
Among unbiased estimators, the **most efficient** 
has the smallest variance.

marginNotes.text:
A false balance is an abomination to 
the Lord, but a just weight is his delight.
